Now, Q2 may also benefit from the same sales momentum in Q1 - even if management has kept the bar low by staying “more cautious and conservative”. The reason being that not all pull-forward orders are likely to be delivered in Q1, while China stimulus is ongoing and will also continue to buoy sales.

The bigger point of interest is the back half of 2025, particularly with management holding off on full-year guidance for now. On the one hand, pull-forward demand is likely to fade by then. Also, if tariffs do take a toll on Chinese growth, smartphone demand, and by extension, demand for MediaTek’s SOCs, will suffer.

But weaker growth also paves the way for stimulus; the Chinese government’s focus on smartphones to stimulate consumption in round 1 bodes well for round 2, which could see the program expanded. Plus, MediaTek’s lack of US exposure, unlike the many Taiwanese names embedded within Apple (AAPL) or Nvidia's (NVDA) supply chains, means it isn’t as exposed to future (geopolitically motivated) chip restrictions. So, relatively speaking, I’m a lot more upbeat on the MediaTek full-year outlook.

On the ‘Edge AI’ Opportunity

Now, there’s a lot more to MediaTek than its near-term appeal. As I highlighted previously, the proliferation of generative AI has opened up some very compelling addressable market opportunities. Top of mind here is ‘edge AI’ (i.e., a shift toward processing locally, as opposed to the cloud), which looks poised to accelerate alongside emerging on-device LLMs like DeepSeek-R1. Echoing management on the Q4 call, “the trend is democratizing the AI and it will spread more for the more average users. And that's good from the edge devices point of view”.

To be clear, it’s still far too early to underwrite ‘edge AI’ triggering a major replacement cycle anytime soon. But things are moving in the right direction and MediaTek confirming that its flagship Dimensity 9400 chipset “already supports” the on-device DeepSeek model is very positive for this Asian ‘picks and shovels’ play.

In the meantime, management’s industry-level guidance, sans AI-driven replacement cycle, is for this year’s global smartphone sales “to increase at a similar pace as 2024”. This seems about right, though for MediaTek, the confluence of China stimulus (note MediaTek is “highly concentrated in China”), as well as continued market share gains at the premium end, sets things up nicely for another year of above-industry growth.

ASIC Business in Focus After Major Google Win

Also worth watching at MediaTek’s upcoming earnings report is guidance for its ASIC business - a much smaller revenue contributor currently vs smartphones but one with a lot of longer-term growth potential. Recall from last quarter that management guided for ASIC sales to “contribute a sizable annual revenue starting in 2026” or, more specifically, “exceeding TWD 1 billion size”. A very solid number, to be sure, but still a slight delay vs the earlier “late-2025 or 2026” guidance.

Expect a big ASIC guidance change, however, in light of news about a partnership with Google (GOOG) on its next AI chip. To be clear, Broadcom (AVGO) remains the #1 player here, both in terms of market share and margins; this breakthrough is nonetheless a great start for MediaTek’s ASIC expansion – even if this initial business comes with below par margins.

What is most important here, in my view, is MediaTek being in a position to capitalize on longer-term share gain opportunities in this >$45bn addressable market (management’s estimate). Thus, if we do get positive on updates next quarter on progress toward its “good share” target, expect the stock to re-rate.

Summing Up

Amid the near-term macro uncertainties, MediaTek stands out as a way to stay relatively defensive on tech while still benefiting from long-term growth opportunities (AI, ASICs, etc.). Not all of these positives are baked into the stock, with its one-year forward P/E hovering in the low-teens and its dividend yield in the ~4% range.

Net-net, I’d stay long – either directly through the over-the-counter listing or indirectly through more liquid US-listed funds like iShares’ MSCI Taiwan ETF (EWT) (see EWT: Improved Taiwan Risk/Reward Heading Into Election Season) or Nomura’s Taiwan Fund (TWN) (see Taiwan Fund: Maximally Levered To Global Tech Trends).

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Rates Spark: The Dust Settles, Angst Remains
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker, Benjamin Schroeder

As the dust settles around USTs, market participants are still alarmed

The volatility in global rates markets seems to have eased somewhat, but the tension in US Treasury bonds can still be felt. So far, nothing in the financial plumbing of markets seems to have broken, which is a sign of resilience after the recent sharp moves. What helps is that the current 10Y UST yield of 4.3% is well within the trading range of the past few years. Remember that the 10Y touched 5% in 2023. Earlier that year, SVB Bank collapsed due to losses in its bond portfolio. Now, financial institutions have got used to the new yield levels for a prolonged period.

Whilst leveraged players in the bond market may have suffered from the sharp swings in USTs, no lasting damage has emerged. The most recently available data on futures positioning did not suggest that the basis trade using USTs was unwinding to a greater degree. The higher volatility in UST swap spreads suggests that a popular trade has suffered here.

Still spooked by recent dynamics, US government officials are trying to appease Treasury markets. The question is whether markets are susceptible to this type of rhetoric against the current risk sentiment with larger macro factors such as inflation and deficit pressures in the backdrop. After Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent assured markets on Monday about the Treasury’s ability to intervene in the UST market if needed, Deputy Treasury Secretary Michael Faulkender mentioned ongoing discussions about the Supplementary Leverage Ratio on Tuesday. Before the tariff announcements, USTs were sensitive to such remarks, as these regulatory changes were expected to materially ease dealer intermediation costs in the UST market, if not boost demand itself. By now, 10y USTs are back to yielding 50bp above SOFR OIS; amid the recent turmoil, this spread went above 60bp. However, before that, the hopes of regulatory change had already helped push it below 40bp.

When diving into the market plumbing of the eurozone, few signs of stress are visible. The spread on riskier eurozone government bonds such as Italian BTPs are slightly elevated compared to pre-liberation day but still below January’s levels. A recent rating upgrade of Italy has certainly helped. The yield on the 10Y Bund at 2.5% is in the middle of the trading range from the past year. The stability of euro markets compared to the US may make it an attractive safe haven to hedge against global uncertainty. As such, we think that Bunds could see healthy demand from global investors in preparation for future turmoil. The latest Bloomberg headlines suggest that EU trade negotiations were making only "scant progress".

Wednesday’s events and market view

The main data focus today is on the US. March retail sales numbers should be very strong, as consumers anecdotally went out and made major purchases ahead of the imposition of tariffs. Auto volumes jumped 10.6% month-on-month based on Wards data, while credit card spending figures also suggest firm demand for appliances and electronics.

With regards to industrial production, this is also closely watched. But business surveys suggest the lack of clarity on the trading environment due to tariffs and fears of potential foreign reprisals and potential consumer boycotts of US exports is causing nervousness. Closely watched will be the speech by Fed Chair Jerome Powell later in the day.

The eurozone will release the final CPI data for March ahead of Thursday’s European Central Bank meeting.

On the heels of a rating upgrade at the end of last week, Italy has mandated banks for the sale of a new 7y bond and a new 30y inflation-linked bond. Germany auctions two 30y Bund lines for €2.5bn.

Outside the eurozone, the UK sells 3y gilts (£1.5bn). The main focus later in the day is on the US Treasury, which auctions US$13bn in 20y bonds, with metrics again eyed closely for any hints of waning demand from foreign investors.
